chunks.text: Rock lobster industry. Yeah, come to the table.

Morning. Morning. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. You are scheduled for 10 minutes. The time is yours. Morning. Morning. Morning, Mr. Chairman and members of the committee. I'm Catherine Malloy, the Chief Executive of Crayate Rock Lobster Industry Association. I've brought along Mark Edwards, who you'll know, for any spicy questions.

We represent the Crayate fishery in Southern New Zealand. Crayate is arguably the most abundant lobster fishery of anywhere in the world, but it wasn't always that way. So if we start with one fishing pot, let's say it's yours, Mr. Chairman. In 1992, if you pulled your pot from the waters of Crayate, you would end up with less than one cray fish for dinner.

Our fishery was in critical condition. Today, you'd pull up over 16 legal sized crays, which is much more than a bag limit. And on a good day, my fishermen tell me you'd often get over 150 cray fish in a pull. This is what we mean by abundance. In Southland we say, if you're a fisher or a diver and you can't catch a cray in Crayate-... you should probably stick to fishing at Pattersons.

This didn't happen because the government mandated it. Our organization, supported by our quota owners, developed a rebuild management procedure that reduced the TACC from 1,150 to 570 tons. We funded the science, we contributed the data, and we held the line to rebuild the fishery to the 1,392 tons it is today.

The rebuild happened because of the tools we're asking you to formalize in this bill. The tools aren't about taking more, they're about managing smarter and faster to ensure the future of our fisheries. Crayate contributes $190 million in export earnings per year. Yes.

But more importantly, we're the 65 fishing crews. We're the young fishermen trying to build a life like their tupuna before them, and we're the small regional communities like Rakiura Stewart Island, where fishing isn't a lifestyle, it's a living. There are four essential parts of this bill for us.

The most important is the recognition of management procedures. Right now, without a management procedure, the TACC is set and can be left unchanged for what can be five years to a decade, regardless of what's happening in the water. In a world of marine heat waves and mortality events, a static TACC is a huge risk, and that uncertainty has very real cost.

Picture a young fisherman sitting across from a bank manager and asking for a loan of $3 million to buy a vessel. The bank manager leans forward. "What guarantee have you got that the fish will even be there in the future?" It's a question he can't answer. We're losing the next generation, not because they don't want to fish, but because the system gives them nothing solid to build a business on.

The management procedures fix that. It's a publicly consulted rule that adjusts the TACC each year based on the most recent data. It's not just CPUE, it's biomass, it's sex ratios, and it's migration patterns. If the biomass increases, then so does the TACC.

If it falls below a threshold, the response is automatic and it's swift. It's disciplined, transparent, and science-based management. Crayate is the proof. We've been running management procedures for 25 years, and they work. Without the amendments, management procedures have no formal legislative home, and without that, there's no guarantee we'll even be allowed one in our next stock assessment.

We simply cannot run New Zealand's best fishery blind. The second tool we need is a formal recognition of industry voluntary measures, particularly catch shelving. Catch shelving is where quota owners voluntarily set aside catch to protect the long-term, um, health of their stocks. That kind of discipline deserves a formal place in law. If you want a proactive industry that supports abundant fisheries, you must give us the tools to enable it.

The third is tiered stock management. The current framework requires the same process for every stock, regardless of how much data exists. Not every fishery can be Crayate. We measured 85,000 fish last year. Others can't do that. Crayate will meet the highest data requirements and- but fisheries with less should still get proactive management rather than being thrown in the too hard basket.

Finally, carry forward, enabling unused catch to be carried into the following season in a limited capacity with the majority of quota ownership and agreement. It's not about gaming the system. It's about flexibility when disruption is beyond our control. When COVID-19 closed China's live seafood market overnight, our fishers couldn't land catch that was swimming in abundance right below them.

The fish was there, the quota was there, the market was not. Quota owners should not permanently lose their catch because of an event they had no, um, part in causing. So our ask is clearEnsure management procedures, recognition of voluntary measures and tiered stock assessments are included. These are not untested theories, they're proven tools that are internationally recognized as best practice, and they're essential in regions of New Zealand where fishing is important and it's not something that people only do if they can't golf.

We went from barely one crayfish per pot to over 16, and we did it with management procedures, voluntary restraint and a commitment to doing the right thing. This bill backs the tools that are already working and gives them a place in law, and we urge the committee to support the bill. Thank you. Thank you. Rachel has a question.

Thank you, Chair. Thank you Catherine, for your submission. Got a question about the management procedures. Mm-hmm. I guess given that they are voluntary and can already be instigated- Yeah... what's the benefit of actually having them in law, given that you can do it anyway? So there's two major benefits. One is it formalizes the consultation process around management procedures, and it gives us a way of being able to bring multiple voices to the table when we're designing the management procedures. The second one is we've seen a lot

of challenge to the use of management procedures, and we're already hearing for our next stock assessment that we're unlikely to get a management procedure. So for us, if the management procedures aren't formalized, there's a real risk that we're only going to have that stock assessment and then a wait until the next stock assessment.

Just to sup on that. Yeah. So essentially the legislation would allow overriding of the industry if the management procedure needed to be put in place. Is that what it is? That's already the case. Yeah. So that can already happen? Yeah. Okay. Yeah. So, we design the management procedures. So our CPUE's got a plateau in it as like a safety net, and then we step up based on increases in the biomass above that. If our management procedure said, "Hey, you deserve an increase," but like what happened in Cray 7, they showed two years' worth of

ability to increase the stock before the minister signed it off. So it's always going to be down to the ministerial discretion, but it gives us a way of designing what we think the risk is. So for Cray 8, our fishermen are really used to fishing at high abundance.

So we need to catch more in a pot than other fisheries around the country. Okay. Yeah. Just to sup on that. So with current management procedures- Yeah... are all fishers within that quota, compelled to stick to what has been decided? Yeah. The management procedure adjusts the TRCC.

So the standard mechanisms in the act apply that proportionally- Right... whether it's a decrease- Yeah... or an increase. As Catherine said, the important thing is to give a recognition to management procedures as a tool and the act that's, the provisions that are in there are actually, we think, quite well designed. We haven't suggested any amendments.

They've got a proper process for their establishment. They've got the ability to step out of them if a management procedure doesn't perform. They're quite a complete tool and it deserves recognition in the statute. So even if they're opposed to the, say you suggest a reduction.

Yeah. Like when we designed- They are compelled to- Absolutely. Yeah. Yeah, yeah. So like our rebuild management procedure in '92, that was two 20% cuts over a four-year period. That management procedure was designed by Cray 8. Okay. So we knew the cuts were going to come if we got that management procedure in, if that makes sense.

Okay. So we designed it that way. Yeah. Thank you. And one last question, Sue. Just quickly. You talk biomass. Does that mean measuring biomass? Is that like- It's like-... using a detector to see how many fish are underwater? No. Biomass as in like if you put all of the fish in a bucket, how messy is it? If that makes sense. Okay, cool. Like what's the actual mass of the biology as in volume.

Right. How do you determine that? So we have a voluntary logbook program. So we measure those 86, 85,000 fish a year for the sex, size, tail width, and their location throughout Cray 8. And then very clever scientists turn that into how many fish they think is in the full area, and then we have a proportion that we can harvest.

And that's just for crayfish, it's not the entire biomass. Yeah, yeah. Just for cray, yep. Yeah. But because we're pottering, we don't catch a lot of other stock. Oh, one other thing. You raised this thing about the interruption with COVID-... and also it happened with, obviously there was significant issues as a result of Gabrielle.

Yeah. So in your mind, is the bill sufficient to manage those- Yeah... processes? Yeah. Like to allow people to carry over their catch? So I think there's a couple of- In certain circumstances... there's a couple of circumstances that Mark's put in his submission that broaden it slightly, but from us, from a Cray 8 perspective, we actually like it being quite tight. We don't want it to be something that people can gamify or roll over if they think they can make more money next month.

We want it to be for things that are out of the fishery's control that don't have an impact on sustainability. Perfect. Thank you very much. Okay, thanks. We've come to time. Thanks. Thank you very much for your submissions. And next up we have Safe

and Emily Hall. Are they online?Supposed to be. Mm-hmm. Morning, Emily. Welcome to Primary Production. You have 10 minutes for your submission, and the time is yours. Fire away. Kia ora koutou. Ko Emily Hall toku ingoa. I'm a campaign manager at SAFE, and we thank you for the opportunity to speak today.

SAFE is Aotearoa, New Zealand's leading animal rights organization, representing tens of thousands of Kiwis who value compassion and expect animal welfare laws to be properly enforced. We oppose the Fisheries Amendment Bill and urge the committee to reject it. Our main concern is that this bill prioritizes commercial and industry interests over transparency, the welfare of animals, environmental protections, and upholding legal obligations.

My first point relates to animal sentience being ignored. Fishes are legally recognized as sentient beings under the Animal Welfare Act 1999, meaning they have the capacity to experience pain, fear, distress, and a sense of wellbeing. However, this bill creates a clear inconsistency between the Fisheries Act and the Animal Welfare Act, treats fishes as commodities rather than animals, does not require fisheries decision-makers to consider fish welfare, permits commercial fishing practices that cause prolonged suffering,

and undermines the New Zealand government's own legal recognition of animal sentience. My second point relates to the bill expanding management flexibility without introducing welfare safeguards, and as a result, practices causing severe and preventable suffering would continue unchecked. This includes prolonged asphyxiation during capture and landing, crushing and barotrauma from rapid depth changes, and handling, gutting, and processing without stunning.

Despite decades of evidence of harm to animals, fisheries legislation does not mandate welfare standards for capture, handling, stunning, or slaughter, and these practices are not meaningfully addressed within fisheries management frameworks. We submit that this constitutes harm by omission. I'd like to briefly touch on bycatch, an issue in New Zealand that exposes major regulatory failure. Non-target fishes are injured during capture, exposed due to ear and pressure trauma, and discarded dead or dying, often without being recorded. In terms of

bycatch, this bill focuses on operational certainty rather than recognizing bycatch injury and death as an animal welfare failure. Whilst fish farming is not primarily governed by the Fisheries Act, this act does cover some aspects relating to this industry, and I'd like to speak to this for a moment, because intensive fish farming is simply underwater factory farming.

There are significant welfare issues associated with fish farming, but this bill frames those issues as economic or environmental issues rather than animal welfare issues. Large-scale mortality events often occur without investigation, any follow-up welfare assessment, or consequences for those responsible, which, quite frankly, would be unheard of in any other farming sector.

In terms of transparency and accountability, this bill reduces public scrutiny, restricts access to onboard camera footage, limits transparency around animal welfare outcomes, and leans on voluntary industry-led measures over enforceable standards, which in turn undermines public trust, consumer confidence, and perhaps most importantly, New Zealand's international reputation on animal welfare.

The bottom line here is that transparency must be central, not optional. We are also seeing these same issues reflected in parallel policy settings, including the fast-tracking of large-scale fish farming projects. That process is characterized by limited participation, reduced transparency, and exclusion of fish welfare expertise, despite clear and well-documented welfare risks.

Fish farming developments can be advanced through a framework that effectively sidelines animal welfare considerations at the point of decision-making. Taken together with this bill, that signals a broader policy direction that prioritizes industry over the welfare of animals, environmental protections, and democratic public scrutiny.

The International Aquatic Animal Alliance, of which SAFE is a member, provided a submission on behalf of 174 organizations, warning from an incredibly well-informed, expert international perspective that this bill will weaken marine protections, reduce transparency, increase reliance on industry self-reporting, expand discarding allowances, and further marginalize aquatic animal welfare.

The submission from this international alliance once again called for the rejection of this bill or, at minimum, substantial amendment. My final point relates to precedence and a perceived abuse of democratic process. Sadly, the process undertaken for this bill has mirrored repeated use by the coalition government of urgency-style processes for non-urgent legislation and therefore risks damaging the democratic process.

While not formally under urgency, the rushed consultation timeframe undermines meaningful public engagement and appears to abuse parliamentary urgency.This also raises broader questions about process. Under the current coalition government, we have seen various pieces of legislation that impact the animals and environmental protections progressed at pace despite substantial public opposition.

When that level of public input is not meaningfully reflected, it raises serious questions around transparency and accountability, leading to a significant decrease in public confidence in the system and our democratic processes. In conclusion, we would like to convey that this bill represents a significant regression in fisheries governance.

SAFE urges the Primary Production Committee to reject the Fisheries Amendment Bill in its entirety, to ensure fisheries law aligns with the Animal Welfare Act, to explicitly recognize fish sentience and fisheries management, and commit to a future framework that reduces and ultimately eliminates practices that result in the suffering of fishes.

Fishes are legally recognized as sentient beings, and it is high time our fisheries legislation reflected that reality. Kia ora, thank you, and I welcome any questions. Well, thank you, Emily. Do we have any questions, team? Yes, Mr. Chair. Mark.

Morning, Emily, and thank you, Mr. Chair, for your time. Just cognizant of a few statements you've made this morning in and around the recording. I'm paraphrasing, of course, but I'm confused. Do you believe, and is it the veracity... Or I'll reframe it. Can you prove the veracity of the statement that you said that the recording on commercial boats was inadequate, albeit that there is cameras and routinely MPI officials on board? So do you want to give some feedback on what your thoughts

are because you've made some pretty asserting statements there, Emily. The bill, I believe, intends to reduce the public access to onboard camera footage. So, for example, if a member of the public or someone from an organization such as SAFE were to commit in the future to submit an Official Information Act request for onboard camera footage, we would not be able to receive that information, I believe.

That kind of answers it. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Okay. Any further questions? No, there doesn't seem to be. Look, thank you very much for your time, Emily. I appreciate it. And have a happy day. Thanks, guys. Thank you. Thank you. Right. We're moving on to the McGuinness Institute.

We have Wendy and Josie, I think. Wendy and Greta. Oh, Wendy and Greta. Sorry. We have a PowerPoint as well, which you only sent it to us a few moments ago, so we'll do our best. It's on, it's up. It's up, which I'm very thankful. Thank you.

Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. You have 10 minutes and the time is yours. Thank you very much. Okay. Hello, everybody. Look, firstly, Greta, introducing. She's our fact checker and one of the researchers at the institute.

So she's here to answer any tricky questions. Right. Basically, we're after durable public policy practice. So we don't want to see things be changed and then changed again in significant ways. That's a very costly approach and creates a lot more uncertainty, which of course, your objective is to reduce the level of uncertainty.

Just wondering if there's a clicker? Just asking for the next slide. Oh, okay. Thank you very much. Okay. Basically, there's some key points I wanted to put in really quickly. Ocean is a living resource. It's not like a mineral asset, and some of the debate that has been made by the Crown and ministers and so forth is, for me, they're sort of forgetting why we're here.

Ocean function is an integrated ecosystem, so it's not like dairy farming. It's about the precautionary approach and the concept of the full scientific certainty should not delay action to prevent serious harm. So in other words, uncertainty is used to reduce harm, not to basically increase harm.

The ocean is a shared common good. It's not privately owned like other resources. So that's an issue. It's under growing threat from climate change, and I think this is really forgotten. New Zealand is actually incredibly large in terms of when you look at the jurisdiction globally. We are considered to be the fourth or fifth largest, and we have a per capita responsibility that is probably second to none.

It's massive. And we also have societies around the world that don't have citizenship and then also don't have rights to water. So we actually have a massive responsibility and leadership role here that is, I think, misunderstood. Now, the next slide that you have up here is basically just showing I look for durable public policy. So I look for historySo where is the history for the Minister for the Environment and Conservation in this dialogue? So you go back through the impact statements, regulatory impact statement, departmental disclosure statements.

Even though oceans and fisheries is meant to be, the description is sustainability of those resources, none of the ministers or departments that are responsible for sustainability are involved in any of the impact statements. So your credibility is diminished because you haven't invited their comments.

And if you'd invited their comments, they're the ones that have the expertise on sustainability. And that's why one of the reasons we actually strongly advocate that the Minister of Oceans is not the same minister for fisheries. We think they need to be decoupled so that you clearly, in terms of government going forward, that you actually have a way of managing that.

This slide here looks terribly messy, but I think a really-- Sorry, the one with the table. Mm-hmm. Is a really important point. You talk about in the explanatory note in the act as though you've got two variables, but you're actually dealing with three variables of uncertainty. So this indicates to you that if you look at the eight options of those variable things, you've only got one that is in high information stock.

You've got three that are medium, and you've got four that are low. And if that's your intention, that's fine, but I actually think that's really problematic. Then the next bit after that, this is a technical point, is that you then have whether the trend is increasing, whether the trend is the same or decreasing. So you've got three other further options in that. So the permutations that you're dealing with, you're putting forward in this approach, actually has a lot of risk. I didn't want to go any further, but I just wanted to

explain that the way that the bill in the explanatory note is written is not technically correct in terms of the variables and the different values you can have for each variable. And I think that's really problematic. The next thing is, I really want business to have certainty.

I want New Zealand to flourish. I want the economic development. The problem is that we're increasingly having to deal with uncertainty. So your ability to create this bill to create uncertainty is actually challenged by the very environment that we're heading into. Does that make sense?

So it's like you want to be here, but in reality, the uncertainty is so much that you can't be here. For example, with that table, you could argue that all of those or every stock is uncertain. And you might get another minister in the next term or the term after that that will make that assessment, and that will create a lot more uncertainty for the fishing industry and for everyone else. So my point being is that you've got to be really careful how you design systems. The next point is around

the power to the minister. Now, I've always argued that you want to make policy decisions central with ministers. You don't want to make specific decisions, operational decisions with ministers, because once again, that creates a weakness in the system.

So you want to basically push down operational stuff, and Parliament and MPs should be strategic. And there's a real risk with that. So, I've put a little screenshot about concern over change owns favoring commercial industry mates. I don't know whether that's true or not. I don't really care.

The fact is the uncertainty has been created, the perception has been created, and you need to design the bill in such a way to take that uncertainty out, not just for this minister, but for future ministers. So I've made a suggestion, something like the minister must, on an annual basis, publish a list of all fish, seafood, and other gifts received from commercial fishers. And I can say the current minister has lots of them and has has a very good appetite, as do I.

The checks and balances I wanted to put forward was expanding the scope of actions and offenses. So the penalties, I think that's still very weak. Establishing more marine reserves. We have got this commitment that we have made, going back to my earlier point.

One way to deliver a more sustainable approach is to create much more marine reserves and protect. That is a mechanism and tool that's within your power and ability to do. Another check and balance is Schedule 8B, I think is incredibly narrow. I put some suggestions in there.

I actually worked on a contract with Treasury many years ago. Treasury is anything to do with numbers and impacts. Department of Internal Affairs, obviously local industry, Audit New Zealand, Parliamentary Commission for the Environment, Ministry for the Environment, Ombudsman, Climate Change Commission.

They are a few, but they should be on that list because these are the easy hits, the checks and balances that you can put in the system to create durable public policy and informed officials across the public service so that you get a bill that works.Thank you.

Very good. Suz Redmayne. Hi. Thank you very much for your submission and for your comprehensive written submission. That was great. I just was interested in your comment about why focus on exports when they benefit such a small number of businesses which are owned by international shareholders.

Do you not agree that exports benefit all New Zealanders, like by bringing money in from overseas? We're not getting rich selling houses to each other. We actually need money coming in from overseas. We don't. I know. I suppose, it was put to me another way. If I had to choose between minerals and mining versus fishing, I would choose minerals and mining.

And I hate to say that, but that's my view. Because you've got a fixed product that you can manage that's not living. It's not as vulnerable as a fishing industry. So to go to your point, we have a lot of companies that operate in New Zealand in- Yeah, I have something... that are overseas, um- I can be very quick.

Yeah. Oh, cool. Oh, Suz had us up, so yeah. I- Oh, no. You go. I was just going to say, though, surely, in terms of carbon emissions, though, this is a more sustainable option in terms of- Yes... you know, as an industry. Yes, exactly. I think there's trade-offs. I think the problem for me is that the bill is being looked at in a very narrow scope, and you haven't had the sustainability, but the bill and the act requires. I mean, sorry, as in the purpose of the portfolio requires.

And so, yeah, so there is a real need to bring more expertise across all facets. Teanaru had a question very quickly. Yeah. It was just around your comments around, and thank you very much for the very precise submission. It was just your comments around the minister consolidating powers.

And so, are you suggesting that we shouldn't have that, or would that be your preferred option, or are these other things that you're talking about to sort of try to temper that? I just struggled to hear that part. Yeah, you've got very poor sound quality, Teanaru.

Yeah. It was just around the consolidation of the powers with the minister. You made a comment about that, and I was just wondering, is your preference for that to be removed from the legislation, or what's the next best step there? Powers of?

He's, the powers of the minister, what's your- Yep... preference? Yeah. Oh, sorry. Thank you. Definitely, we've always taken a view that power in too few hands is risky. You want checks and balances. But more importantly, when the minister has operational power, you create risk. What you want to do is push it down.

It's a little bit different. It's like the chairman and the chief executive. So the chairman wants to think strategically. He employs or doesn't employ the chief executive. That is what the way that government should work. MPs should think strategically. They should be asking questions, like Suzy, around, where does the economic value for New Zealand be?

Have we set up the systems right? The minister should not be the system. Thank you. Thanks very much. We've come to time. I appreciate it. Sorry, I could've keep going. Oh, we love it. Thank you very much. Thank you so much. Appreciate your submission. Really appreciate it. Cheers. Thank you very much.

We're going to move on to Young Ocean Explorers, who are via Zoom. And I think we have a Steve Hathaway. Is that you, Steve? It is. Good morning. Hey, thank you for having me. Good morning, Steve. You've got 10 minutes. Away you go. Wonderful. Hey, I'm Steve Hathaway, founder of Young Ocean Explorers.

I've spent over 40 years, and many thousands of hours, underneath the surface of the ocean around New Zealand. I'm a spear fisherman. I'm also a professional underwater cameraman. For the last 18 years, I've been fortunate to film for the likes of Sir David Attenborough with Blue Planet II.

Having his speaking over my footage is definitely a life highlight. Regarding Young Ocean Explorers, it was formed 13 years ago with my daughter Riley, who's now 25. She was 12 then. The whole idea was creating stories to inspire this whole generation of Kiwi kids to bring alive our underwater story of New Zealand. And so initially it was a TV show, but now we have an online platform that's used by thousands of classrooms around New Zealand. Hundreds of thousands of tamariki have used our content. It's interesting, we've had a whole generation that have come through it now, which is

really, really cool. Our goal is to ensure we have a thriving ocean for future generations, and we're on the coalface with Kiwi kids. We've personally given presentations to over 150,000 kids around Aotearoa, and we're hearing regularly that this generation of kids are really concerned about the planet and the ocean they're inheriting.

And this is part of what I want to speak to this morningThey're the ones that are inheriting this planet. They're affected by the decisions we're making today. Our big question for this bill on behalf of these children is, does this bill help enable to give a thriving ocean for future generations? Does this bill allow the health of the ocean to go on an upward trajectory?

Does it ensure it is a better and healthier ocean for them? And this is something I think we really need to consider in all our decisions. We think it doesn't. I want to take a little bit of a backward step just to give some context about what we believe and our worldview as an organization to give context to why we oppose this bill. New Zealand is world renowned for its beauty and its incredible flora and fauna, our keas, kiwis, and kauri trees. Our national identity is built around this. I've just been at the Raglan Surf Contest in the weekend.

New Zealand Tourism is a sponsor, and they're using the 100% Pure brand. It is a world-renowned brand and a magnificent country. We have our magnificent national parks that ensure that we and future generations get to experience firsthand the closest glimpse of what much of New Zealand used to look like.

We have Predator Free 2050, where we're bringing back the bird song all around Aotearoa. And I'm amazed to see what's happening in our capital, hoping to be the first capital in the world to be predator-free, and they're releasing kiwi there. Audacious goals and vision that even if not fully achieved, they will definitely leave a better legacy for future generations.

Yet amazingly, most of New Zealand is actually ocean, about 93% of it, and it's thought over 80% of our natives live there. This is just as much part of New Zealand and our identity. New Zealand underwater was once an incredible place of abundance.

It was not uncommon to catch hāpuku from near the coastline. New Zealand's legendary marine storyteller, he was like the Cousteau of New Zealand, Wade Dove, said to me that crayfish, he is one of the original divers, crayfish would have been as common as cockroaches. A very old friend of mine told me that he wouldn't stop to have a fish at 90 Mile Beach until he saw the ocean was pink, where he knew there's enough snapper that he would get a good feed.

These days are long gone and something I've never experienced, but I've seen significant changes in over 40 years in the ocean. I've seen crayfish and scallops in abundance and something that we thought would never run out. They should be our God-given right as Kiwis to harvest. But now in Northland, we're not allowed to take either of them, as numbers are so diminished.

How has it been so poorly managed that it's got to this place? We have underwater deforestation happening on a grand scale. Our kelp forests disappearing along with the diversity of life found within them. If this was happening on the land, we would not tolerate it. I've heard criticism pointing the finger at previous generations that hunted moa to extinction, and of course, this would be incomprehensible on the land in this day and age. Yet in the last 30 years, we've virtually brought New Zealand's underwater version of the moa to

extinction. The world's second-largest fish, the basking shark, which in the '90s was still seen in schools of hundreds off the Kaikoura and Canterbury coast. But now through accidental capture or bycatch, they're virtually extinct. This animal is an animal that this generation of kids will probably never see. Our forefathers would have found it incomprehensible that our fisheries could be so mismanaged that it's come to this.

We've heard the saying, "Without a vision, the people perish." We would say neglect having a vision for a healthy ocean, and its life will perish, and this is what we're currently experiencing. Unlike the land, which has a vision for abundance, there is no vision for a healthy ocean. It's quite obvious about this in New Zealand.

There is a huge disparity between how we view and treat the ocean compared to the land. As an organization, we want the future of our ocean to truly be great for future generations, but we have to make some very intentional decisions to get there. We look to have a vision for our oceans, and we need to have a vision for our ocean and an ambitious goal of what it will look like in the future.

New Zealand's ocean is just as much part of New Zealand as the land, and it's quite possibly that it's every bit as unique underwater, if not more so, with the amount of natives that we have there. As an organization, we're not against commercial fishing, as there are plenty of Kiwis that can't fish, but we're against commercial interests being put above general public interests and being put above general New Zealanders.

The drive to make decisions that place more importance on profit above what is best for the general public, we're against this stuff. This bill does not address some of the serious underlying issues that has brought New Zealand to this point or any of the destructive techniques we currently use. Our first concern is that the bill represents a significant departure from ecosystem-based and precautionary management.

The environmental principles in the Fisheries Act exists because of the inextricable link between fisheries and wider impacts on marine ecosystems. Put simply, healthy fisheries depend on and are a part of healthy ecosystems. Yet this bill fundamentally changes how environmental concerns are handled in fisheries decisions.

It's moving away from caution towards maximizing use, even when there is uncertainty. And uncertainty matters in the ocean. Ecosystems don't come with instruction manuals. When we push too far, the consequences don't always arrive all at once. They arrive in complex shifts in the balance over time, and they can lead to swift collapse, often irreversibly.

We're already seeing signs of this in New Zealand waters.The bill shifts this balance by narrowing the role of environmental considerations, weakening obligations for setting catch limits for data poor stocks, locking in multi-year catch decisions, and allowing for in-season catch increases for highly variable stocks.

Other changes, as detailed in our submission, further reduce precaution and prioritize utilization and efficiency over ecological resilience, and we recommend they are removed. The second concern we want to raise is the way the bill substantially reduces transparency, public accountability, and public participation.

This is not just about science, it's also about trust. Our fisheries are a publicly owned resource. They are not owned by a few, but they belong to all New Zealanders. And with a shared resource comes a fundamental principle. We all deserve a say, we all deserve visibility, and we all deserve accountability. Yet this bill moves us in the opposite direction.

Onboard cameras have dramatically improved transparency and trust in the system, increasing both reporting and data availability. However, the bill excludes camera footage from the Official Information Act and criminalizes the release with severe penalties of up to $50,000. Not only is this overly punitive, but it is completely unnecessary. The OIA is a key mechanism, obviously, for public accountability and management of a shared resource.

Existing OIA protections already manage for privacy and commercial sensitivities. There's been no evidence of misuse under the OIA, and removing access to footage further erodes transparency, which in turn erodes confidence and trust. This provision should be removed. Further, the bill reduces consultation requirements, leaving it to the minister to decide whether to seek public input on non-regulatory measures proposed by quota owners and variations to catch limits. These changes should also be removed.

The proposed 20 working day limit on judicial review is completely unreasonable in our view. The timeframe ignores the highly complex and technical nature of fisheries management decision, and also just goes against the officials' advice of three to six months. So this is a clear attempt to limit legitimate challenges to fisheries decisions from being properly considered and should be removed.

The final concern we want to raise is the process through which this bill was developed. Reforms of this scale should be built through transparent, inclusive, and robust policy development. That's not happened here. The proposals were largely shaped through consultation with or engagement with commercial fishing interests, with limited involvement from fishery scientists, environmental groups, customary and recreational fishers, or the wider public.

As you'll be aware, when consulted on 2025, around 25,000 submissions opposed the proposals, demonstrating significant public concern. But when it was introduced in March, it reflected almost none of that feedback or genuine response to concerns raised. This has understandably undermined confidence in the bill and the process behind it.

We've seen, as Jamie mentioned, considerable public backlash, particularly around the minimum size provision, followed by the government reversing course on that. In our view, that kind of raises broader questions about whether all aspects of the bill have been properly scrutinized before introduction. And even with the one-week extension, the timeframe for select committee submissions remains wholly inadequate for legislation of this complexity and significance.

For reforms this significant, the process has simply not been good enough. Good fisheries management depends on public trust. That trust is weakened when major reforms are rushed, consultation is limited, and public feedback appears to have little influence on the outcomes. To conclude, a precautionary, science-led, and transparent fisheries management system is essential to minimize harm to our marine species and habitats and rebuild public trust in the system.

While we have identified amendments that should be removed, simply fixing individual provisions alone is not enough. Given the scale and significance of the proposed changes, the strength of public opposition, and the poor process by which it was advanced, WWF is of the view that the best path forward is to effectively restart the policy development process.

That should include an independent review of the management system, including the quota management, followed by the development of revised proposals through full and inclusive public consultation process. These reforms risk taking us backward at a time when increasing pressures exist on our marine environment. This bill is too important to rush through without getting it right.

Future generations will live with the consequences of the decisions made here. It is in our economic and ecological long-term interest to hit the restart on this bill to actually ensure sustainable fisheries that support both healthy marine ecosystems and stable future livelihoods.
